question_title: Will total 2026 DeFi hack losses exceed $1 billion on DefiLlama by May 31, 2026?
summary: DeFi hack losses for 2026 YTD stand at $840.8M as of May 24, needing $159.2M more in 7 days to cross $1B. At the current daily rate (~$1.7M/day), only ~$12M would accumulate — far short. Reaching $1B essentially requires a single mega-hack exceeding $159M or an extraordinary cluster of large hacks. Monte Carlo simulation bootstrapping from 2026's empirical hack size distribution gives ~9.3% probability. Parametric models based on historical mega-hack frequencies (3-5/year) give 3-5.5%. May 2026 has been relatively quiet compared to April's catastrophic $635M. The primary YES path is a DPRK-scale mega-hack, which is possible but unlikely in any given 7-day window. Final estimate: ~10%, reflecting the empirical bootstrap result adjusted slightly upward for reporting lag and the elevated 2026 threat environment.

factors.description: Current YTD total is $840.8M (from DefiLlama API), needing $159.2M more in just 7 days — a massive gap that requires a rare mega-hack event
Normal daily hack rate (~$1.8M/day excluding mega-hacks) would only generate ~$12.6M in 7 days, far short of the $159M needed
2026 is an elevated threat year with DPRK/Lazarus Group conducting $575M+ in attacks across two mega-hacks; threat environment remains active
Empirical Monte Carlo bootstrap from 2026 hack size distribution (500K simulations) shows ~9.3% probability of 7-day losses exceeding $159M
Reporting lag of 4-16 days observed in 2026 means some hacks may have already occurred but not yet appeared on DefiLlama
Status quo strongly favors NO — May 2026 has averaged only $1.7M/day over 23 days with no hack above $11.5M

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will 2026 DeFi hack losses exceed $1 billion by May 31?

**Probability: 10%**

### Key Analysis

| Factor | Status |
|--------|--------|
| Current YTD total | **$840.8M** (from DefiLlama API, 84 hacks) |
| Gap to $1B threshold | **$159.2M** needed in 7 days |
| Normal daily rate | ~$1.7-1.8M/day → only ~$12M expected |
| Monte Carlo estimate | **9.3%** (500K simulations, empirical bootstrap) |

### Why 10% (mostly NO)

The fundamental challenge is arithmetic: at the current pace, only ~$12M would accumulate in the remaining 7 days, leaving the total at ~$853M — well short of $1B. Reaching the threshold **requires a mega-hack exceeding $159M**, which would rank among the top 5-10 DeFi hacks of all time.

**Paths to YES (~10%):**
- Single mega-hack >$159M (similar to April's Kelp or Drift events): ~6-7%
- Multiple large hacks combining to >$159M: ~2-3%  
- Undiscovered/unreported hacks with reporting lag: ~1%

**Paths to NO (~90%):**
- Continuation of May's quiet pattern ($1.7M/day) — the overwhelmingly likely scenario

### Key uncertainties
- Whether DPRK/Lazarus Group has another major operation ready (they drove 68.7% of 2026 losses)
- Whether any significant undiscovered hacks already exist but haven't been reported yet (4-16 day reporting lags observed in 2026)
- The true annual rate of >$100M DeFi hacks (small sample, high variance)
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will total 2026 DeFi hack losses exceed $1 billion on DefiLlama by May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43586)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, when checked on May 31, 2026 (UTC), the sum of "Amount lost" across all DeFi hack incidents dated January 1, 2026 through May 31, 2026 (inclusive) listed on the DefiLlama Hacks & Exploits database at https://defillama.com/hacks is at least US$1,000,000,000 (one billion US dollars). To compute, filter the hacks table for incidents with a date in 2026 and sum the "Amount lost" column. Bridge hacks are included if and only if DefiLlama classifies them as DeFi exploits in its database listing (i.e., they appear in the main hacks table; the "Total Value Hacked in DeFi" aggregate excludes the separate "Bridges" aggregate). If DefiLlama is unavailable on May 31, 2026, the value reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) citing DefiLlama data for 2026 YTD DeFi hack losses will be used.
fine_print: For consistency, the figure used is the sum of all 2026-dated incidents in DefiLlama's main Hacks & Exploits table (https://defillama.com/hacks), regardless of subsequent recoveries or returned funds — DefiLlama's listed "Amount lost" at the time of resolution governs. If DefiLlama later revises figures for individual incidents, the values as displayed on May 31, 2026 are what count.

factors.description: Current YTD total is $840.8M, needing $159.2M more in just 7 days — a massive gap requiring a rare mega-hack event
Normal daily hack rate (~$1.8M/day excluding mega-hacks) would only generate ~$12.6M in 7 days, far short of the $159M needed
2026 has been an elevated year for DeFi hacks with DPRK/Lazarus Group conducting $575M+ in attacks; threat environment remains elevated
Empirical Monte Carlo bootstrap from 2026 hack size distribution shows ~9.3% probability of 7-day losses exceeding $159M
Reporting lag of 4-16 days means some hacks may have already occurred but not yet appeared on DefiLlama
Status quo strongly favors NO — most 7-day windows produce $10-20M in hack losses, not $159M+

input.anchor: Historical base rate: In any random 7-day window in DeFi history, the probability of $159M+ in hack losses is approximately 5-10%, based on empirical frequency of mega-hacks (3-5 per year at >$100M each)
input.assessment:
This question asks whether DeFi hack losses will go from $840.8M to $1B+ in the remaining 7 days of May 2026. The gap is $159.2M. At the normal daily rate (excluding mega-hacks), only ~$12.6M would accumulate — far short. Therefore, YES requires either: (1) a single mega-hack >$159M, or (2) multiple large hacks summing to >$159M. 

The empirical bootstrap simulation (sampling from actual 2026 hack sizes with Poisson arrivals) gives ~9.3% probability. Parametric models using historical mega-hack frequencies (3-5/year) give 3-5.5%. The bootstrap is more complete as it captures paths involving combinations of medium-sized hacks.

Key considerations: 2026 has been an elevated year driven by DPRK state-sponsored attacks, but the specific targets (Kelp, Drift) have already been exploited. Reporting lag could add some undiscovered losses but unlikely at the $100M+ scale. The 7-day window is very short, which strongly constrains accumulation paths.

My estimate of ~10% balances the empirical bootstrap result with the short time window and the reality that mega-hacks are rare but not impossibly so in the current threat environment.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~7.5% (midpoint of 5-10% range). My 10% estimate is slightly above anchor, reflecting 2026's elevated threat environment. The empirical bootstrap (9.3%) and my estimate (10%) are close, suggesting good calibration. Not hedging toward 50% — the evidence clearly favors NO.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether DPRK/Lazarus Group has another major exploit ready to execute in the next 7 days. 2. Whether any significant hacks have already occurred but haven't been reported yet. 3. The true frequency of >$100M DeFi hacks (small sample size makes this uncertain).
input.tool_audit: DefiLlama API (via research agent): Excellent - provided complete 2026 hack data with 84 incidents totaling $840.8M. DefiLlama web fetch: Partial - showed recent May hacks but couldn't extract full YTD total cleanly. Research agent: Very useful for both API data and rekt.news deep-dive on attack vectors. Monte Carlo simulation: Core analytical tool, provided the 9.3% bootstrap estimate. All tools functioned well.
input.update_triggers: A new mega-hack (>$50M) reported in the next few days would substantially increase the probability. Conversely, continued quiet on the exploit front through May 28-29 would decrease it further.
input.process_reflection: The research agent was extremely effective here — it pulled the DefiLlama API data directly and provided a comprehensive breakdown. The Monte Carlo simulation in the sandbox was the right approach for a threshold question with fat-tailed distributions. The main friction was deciding how to weight the empirical bootstrap (which uses 2026's potentially anomalous distribution) vs. longer historical base rates of mega-hacks. The scaffolding worked well for this question type.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All six factors are grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1** ($840.8M YTD, $159.2M gap): Confirmed by DefiLlama API data showing "Total gross losses: $840,815,935" across 84 hacks. Gap calculation ($1B - $840.8M = $159.2M) is arithmetically correct.
- **Factor 2** (~$1.8M/day normal rate, ~$12.6M in 7 days): Confirmed by code execution output: "Daily rate excluding mega-hacks: $1,837,874/day" and "Expected from normal hacks in 7 days: $12,865,116."
- **Factor 3** (DPRK/Lazarus $575M+): Confirmed by rekt.news research entries for KelpDAO (~$290-293M, DPRK-attributed) and Drift Protocol ($285.26M, DPRK-attributed). Combined total matches.
- **Factor 4** (Monte Carlo 9.3%): Confirmed by code execution output: "P(7-day losses >= $159M) = 0.0929 (9.29%)."
- **Factor 5** (reporting lag 4-16 days): Confirmed by documented lags: KelpDAO 6 days, THORChain 6 days, Resolv 16 days, Wasabi 4 days.
- **Factor 6** (most 7-day windows produce $10-20M): Confirmed by May daily rate ($1.7M/day × 7 = $11.8M) and normal daily rate ($1.8M/day × 7 = $12.9M).

### Double-Counting — Minor (not actionable)
Factors 1, 2, and 6 all draw on the same underlying evidence: the gap between current YTD total and $1B target, combined with the normal (non-mega-hack) daily rate. Factor 1 says the gap is massive, Factor 2 quantifies why with the daily rate, and Factor 6 restates that most 7-day windows fall far short. These are three expressions of the same argument. Combined logit weight is -3.5, which overstates the independence of this evidence. However, since the conclusion is correct and well-grounded, and the overall probability isn't distorted to an unreasonable degree, this is not actionable.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs are consistent with factor descriptions: negative logits for factors arguing against YES (1, 2, 6); positive logits for factors providing some probability path to YES (3, 4, 5).

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
Assessment concludes ~10%, consistent with factors collectively favoring NO but acknowledging tail risk. The trace notes factor-implied probability is ~7.5% and the final estimate rounds up to 10%, a modest and justified departure.

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are clearly provided (sum of "Amount lost" on DefiLlama hacks table for 2026-dated incidents, gross figures, as displayed on May 31). The agent correctly uses gross $840.8M, not net $820.2M. The agent correctly identifies that bridge hacks count only if listed in the main table. The resolution source (defillama.com/hacks) is correctly consulted.

### Regime-Spanning Data — Minor concern (not actionable)
The Monte Carlo bootstrap samples from the 2026 empirical hack distribution, which includes two anomalous DPRK mega-hacks ($293M, $285M) that were products of specific multi-month campaigns. This inflates the implied per-draw probability of mega-hacks (2/84 ≈ 2.4% per hack). However, the agent explicitly addresses this concern: (1) the counterargument identifies "selection bias in 2026 data" and notes the DPRK operations "represent a specific campaign that has already been executed"; (2) the agent cross-validates with parametric models (3-5.5%) providing a lower bound; (3) the final estimate of 10% doesn't blindly follow the bootstrap. The agent demonstrates awareness of the regime issue.

### Probability Assessment — PASS
The parametric models give 3-5.5%, the bootstrap gives 9.3%, and the agent settles on 10%. My independent estimate based on the evidence would be ~6-8%, weighting the parametric models somewhat more than the bootstrap due to the DPRK campaign concerns. The agent's 10% is slightly generous but within a reasonable range for this type of tail-probability question. The difference (~2-4pp) is not large enough to constitute a meaningful error, especially given that the agent's own confidence is only 0.50.

### Counterargument Quality — PASS
The counterargument is substantive: identifies selection bias in bootstrap data, conditions on May's quiet period ($1.7M/day for 23 days), flags survivorship bias in mega-hack frequency counts (many were bridge hacks categorized separately), and notes the $159M threshold is in the top 5-10 DeFi hacks of all time. Provides specific quantified update triggers. This is a genuine attempt to argue against the forecast.

### Confidence Self-Assessment — PASS
0.50 confidence is well-calibrated given the wide spread between parametric (3-5.5%) and bootstrap (9.3%) models, and the inherent difficulty of forecasting rare tail events in a 7-day window.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
Anchor is "5-10% based on empirical frequency of mega-hacks." Final estimate of 10% is at the top of the anchor range but within it.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent claims tools worked well. Ground truth: 10 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). No discrepancy.

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
No worldview forecasts exist for this question or related DeFi hack quantities. The worldview research entries (defillama-2026-defi-hack-losses and major-defi-hacks-exploits-may-2026) are consistent with the factors and assessment. Both are marked "fresh."

## Summary
The forecast is well-constructed with thorough data sourcing from the DefiLlama API, a properly executed Monte Carlo simulation, appropriate cross-validation via parametric models, and honest self-assessment of limitations. There is mild double-counting among factors 1/2/6, but this doesn't distort the overall probability to an unreasonable degree. The 10% estimate is at the generous end of the evidence range (my estimate would be ~7%) but not meaningfully wrong. No errors warrant a warn or fail.
